beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: A ten-minute animated video can consume over a thousand hours of work. A ten-minute podcast takes maybe ten. One formula explains exactly where every hidden hour goes.

card.copy: The Equation Hidden Inside Every Animated Second

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A ten-minute animated video can consume over a thousand hours of work. A ten-minute podcast takes maybe ten. One formula explains exactly where every hidden hou

beats.narration_text: Ten minutes of runtime. Ten minutes to watch. The production cost seems like it should scale with the runtime. But animation has a multiplier hiding inside every second that most viewers never think about.

production_viz.label: 10 min runtime vs 1000+ hours production. The hidden multiplier.

beats.narration_text: 24 frames per second. One second of animation is 24 individual drawings. Ten minutes is 600 seconds. 600 times 24 = 14,400 individual frames. Each one is a complete, finished image — not a copy of the previous one.

card.copy: 10 minutes × 60 sec/min × 24 frames/sec = 14,400 drawings. At professional pace, how many hours is that?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: 24 frames per second. One second of animation is 24 individual drawings. Ten minutes is 600 seconds. 600 times 24 = 14,400 individual frames. Each one is a comp

beats.narration_text: Professional animation rate: roughly 4 minutes of artist time per finished frame, for moderately complex character animation. That's not counting cleanup, coloring, or compositing. Just the raw drawing.

production_viz.label: ~4 min/frame professional rate — raw drawing only, before cleanup/color/composite

beats.narration_text: 14,400 frames times 4 minutes per frame = 57,600 minutes = 960 hours. Just for the animation drawings alone. At 40 hours per week, that's 24 weeks of one animator's full time — for 10 minutes of video.

production_viz.label: 14,400 × 4 min = 960 hours. 24 weeks full-time. For 10 minutes.

beats.narration_text: Add overhead: research 50 hours, script 30 hours, music 40 hours, narration recording 20 hours, editing and compositing 100 hours, color and cleanup 200 hours. Total: roughly 1,400 hours. One person, full time: 35 weeks, nearly 9 months, for 10 minutes.

The formula: Hours ≈ (Seconds × fps × minutes per frame) / 60 + overhead. The 'seconds × 24' multiplier is invisible at playback speed — you don't perceive 14,400 separate decisions, you just see motion. That invisibility is why people underestimate animation cost.

production_viz.label: Hours = (Sec × fps × min/frame) / 60 + overhead. The invisible multiplier.

beats.narration_text: This is why animation studios have entire teams. A feature film at 24 fps × 90 minutes × 60 = 129,600 frames. Even at a generous 2 minutes per frame with assembly-line division of labor: 4,320 hours of drawing time. Spread across 50 animators: 86 hours each — but this is just raw animation, before everything else.

production_viz.label: Feature film: 129,600 frames → 4,320 hr drawing ÷ 50 animators = 86 hr each. Just drawing.

beats.narration_text: Digital animation and rigging reduce per-frame cost significantly. A rigged 3-D character moving through a scene might cost 30 seconds of render time per frame rather than 4 minutes of drawing. But the frame count doesn't change. 14,400 renders, each potentially taking minutes or hours on a farm.

production_viz.label: 3D rigging: 30 sec/frame render vs 4 min/frame draw. Frame count unchanged: still 14,400.

beats.narration_text: The formula: Hours = Seconds × 24 × (minutes per frame) / 60. For 10 minutes at 4 minutes per frame: 960 animation hours. Plus 440 overhead = 1,400 total. One second hides 24 drawings. One minute hides 1,440. Ten minutes hides 14,400.

card.copy: Hours = (Sec × 24 × min/frame) / 60 + overhead. 14,400 frames. 960 animation hours. 1,400 total. One second hides 24 drawings.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The formula: Hours = Seconds × 24 × (minutes per frame) / 60. For 10 minutes at 4 minutes per frame: 960 animation hours. Plus 440 overhead = 1,400 total. One s

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
